title: Names and labels
why.no-class: There is still no label. The two bikes are of one kind — if you want to style them the same tomorrow, you must call them the same today. class="bike" on both.
why.id-repeated: You gave the same `id` to two things. An `id` is a PERSONAL name — unique on the page. If two things share a name, nobody knows which one you mean. For things of one kind you use `class`.
why.not-closed: A tag is open and never closed.
blocks.0.text: Three paragraphs. The first two are bikes for sale. The third is a note about delivery. To the browser all three are the same thing: a paragraph.
blocks.1.text: Tomorrow you will want the bikes to look one way and the note another. But how will you tell it which is which?
blocks.4.text: `class` is a label. You put it on everything of one kind. Here: "these are bikes".
blocks.5.text: One thing can carry several labels at once, separated by spaces: class="bike sold". And one label can sit on a hundred things.
blocks.8.text: `id` is different. It is a PERSONAL name — only one thing on the entire page may carry it.
blocks.9.text: Like an ID card number: two people cannot have the same one.
blocks.11.items.0: `class` — a kind. Can repeat. "All the bikes."
blocks.11.items.1: `id` — a name. Only once. "That one bike."
blocks.12.text: If you are unsure — use `class`. It covers ninety percent of cases.
blocks.14.text: Neither does anything on its own. Run the page — nothing changes. No colour, no spacing.
blocks.15.text: They are handles. CSS grabs them to style. JavaScript grabs them to change things. And you add them today so you can do that tomorrow.
blocks.16.text: One thing `id` does on its own
blocks.17.text: There is one case where `id` earns its keep immediately, with no CSS and no JavaScript.
blocks.18.text: A link to `#top` takes you to the thing with that `id` — on the same page, with no reload. That is where tables of contents, "back to top" buttons, and long articles with sections come from.
blocks.19.text: And that is why `id` must be unique: if two things are called "top", the browser does not know where to take you.
blocks.21.text: Here is the mistake you will keep making for years: name by MEANING, not by appearance.
blocks.22.text: class="red-text" looks sensible today. Tomorrow the boss wants the price green. So either you rename everything, or you call it "red" and make it green — and everyone reading the code will wonder if it is a bug.
blocks.23.text: class="price" has no such problem. A price stays a price, whatever you do to it.
blocks.24.text: Your turn. Give the two bikes a shared label.
